coredata.dc:title: Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yaoping
Yang, Jing
Tian, Jiangwei
Jia, Li
Yu, Jun-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Proper disposal of waste frying oil (WFO) is an important waste-management concern. In this paper, we develop a facile method to reuse WFO for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-C-dots) with the assistance of concentrated sulfuric acid. The as-synthesized S-C-dots are uniform in size and show partial disordered graphite-like structure. Different from the doping-free or nitrogen-doped carbon dots, the S-C-dots perform a strong ultraviolet emission at 378nm due to successful sulfur-doping. Noticeably, the S-C-dots exhibit a distinct pH-sensitive feature and the intensity of photoluminescence increases linearly in the pH range from 3 to 9. Furthermore, possessing fascinating optical properties, high photostability, and low cytotoxicity, the S-C-dots have served as fluorescent probes for cell imaging.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots from waste frying oil. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the S-C-dots. Insets in (a) and (b): size distribution and SAED pattern of the S-C-dots.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of the S-C-dots.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C1s spectrum, (c) O1s spectrum, and (d) S2p spectrum of the S-C-dots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the S-C-dots. Insets in (a): photographs of the aqueous solution of the C-dots under daylight (left) and UV light (right). Inset in (b): normalized PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the S-C-dots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra of the S-C-dots dispersion at different pH values (from 2 to 12) with excitation at 380nm. Inset in (a): PL intensity of the S-C-dots dispersion at different pH values (from 2 to 12). (b) A linearrelationship between PL intensity and pH (from 3 to 9). Inset in (b): the reversible pH-response of the PL behavior of the S-C-dots measured by tuning pH from 3 to 9 and then from 9 to 3. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) A Bright-field microphotograph of Hela cells. (b) A confocal fluorescence microphotograph of Hela cells incubated with the S-C-dots in a concentration of 100μgmL−1 for 1h (λ ex =405nm). (c) An overlay image of (a) and (b). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing 210029, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Proper disposal of waste frying oil (WFO) is an important waste-management concern. In this paper, we develop a facile method to reuse WFO for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-C-dots) with the assistance of concentrated sulfuric acid. The as-synthesized S-C-dots are uniform in size and show partial disordered graphite-like structure. Different from the doping-free or nitrogen-doped carbon dots, the S-C-dots perform a strong ultraviolet emission at 378nm due to successful sulfur-doping. Noticeably, the S-C-dots exhibit a distinct pH-sensitive feature and the intensity of photoluminescence increases linearly in the pH range from 3 to 9. Furthermore, possessing fascinating optical properties, high photostability, and low cytotoxicity, the S-C-dots have served as fluorescent probes for cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing population of the world has accelerated the food consumption and therewith produced million tons of waste frying oil (WFO) [1]. The disposal of such huge quantities of WFO is a major concern. Generally, WFO is poured down the drain, resulting in problems for waste water treatment plants and energy loss, or integrated into the food chain through animal feeding, thus becoming a potential cause of human health problems [2]. Several reuses of WFO have been demonstrated including soap manufacture [3] and biodiesel production [1]. However, besides these recycles, is there no other use of WFO? The answer is absolutely yes.

Carbon dots (C-dots), a rising star of fluorescent nanomaterials, with tunable photoluminescence, robust chemical inertness, low environmental hazard, and excellent biocompatibility [4,5], have attracted tremendous attention due to their promising applications in bioimaging [6–8], drug delivery [9], ink [10–12], sensors [13–16], optoelectronics [17–19], and photocatalysis [20–22]. Since their discovery by purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes [23], various precursors including graphite [24–26], graphene [18,22,27], C60 [28,29], multiwalled carbon nanotubes [30], saccharides [19,31–33], amino acid [34,35], citric acid [36–38], tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) [39,40], lauryl gallate [41], candle soot [42], natural gas soot [43], food [44], biomass [10,45–50], and so on, have been employed for the preparation of C-dots with diverse synthetic strategies. In terms of saving energy, protectingtheenvironment, and reducing production cost, the reuse of the waste such as candle soot and natural gas soot as raw carbon sources towards C-dots is more favorable. Despite some impressive advances, the large-scale utilization of the waste resources for fabricating monodisperse C-dots by facile methods is still a great challenge.

On the other hand, heteroatom-doping into C-dots has been actively pursued for effectively tuning the intrinsic properties of C-dots. Nitrogen-doped C-dots (N-C-dots) are widely reported and the significant enhancement of photoluminescence quantum yield has been achieved by nitrogen-doping [12,34,35,49–51]. However, the development of sulfur-doped C-dots (S-C-dots) is extremely limited and the impacts of sulfur-doping need further investigation [52–54].

Herein, we demonstrate the first use of WFO as a precursor for one-step synthesis of S-C-dots with concentrated sulfuric acid carbonization. Due to the strong dehydrating and oxidizing properties, concentrated sulfuric acid has been used to carbonize different carbon sources for synthesis of C-dots. For example, Peng and Travas-Sejdic fabricated luminescent C-dots first by dehydration of carbohydrates with concentrated sulfuric acid, followed by nitric acid treatment and amine-terminated compounds passivation [55]. This route was too complicated andlabor-intensive. Recently, Sun and co-workers synthesized C-dots by carbonization and cutting of hair fiber in the presence of concentrated sulfuric acid [53]. However, a long reaction time (24h) and a large amount of concentrated sulfuric acid (100mL) are required, probably because of the difficulty of destroying the robust structure of the hair fiber. Compared with the previous methods, our approach for synthesis of S-C-dots from WFO can be conducted in one step, and it can finish within a few minutes (5min) at a relatively mild temperature (100°C). The synthesis procedure is shown in Fig. 1 . The as-prepared S-C-dots are monodisperse with narrow size distribution and show partial disordered graphite-like structure. It is found that the successful sulfur-doping opens a wide band gap of 3.28eV in C-dots, bringing about an intense ultraviolet emission of 378nm. Such blue-shifted photoluminescence is hardly observed in doping-free or nitrogen-doped C-dots. Remarkably, the S-C-dots have a distinct pH-sensitive photoluminescence in the pH range from 3 to 9 that gives them the potential to serve as optical pH sensors. Furthermore, possessing satisfactory photoluminescence quantum yield, high photostability, and low cytotoxicity, the S-C-dots have been applied as fluorescent probes for cell imaging.

WFO was obtained from the local restaurants. The elemental analysis of the WFO is C 74.57wt%, H 10.85wt%, N 0.01wt%, and S 2.83wt%. Concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) was purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagents Factory (Nanjing, China). 3-(4,5-Dime-thylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Sigma Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). The water used in the experiments was purified through a Millipore water purification system (⩾18MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore). All other reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

S-C-dots were prepared by carbonization of WFO with the assistance of concentrated sulfuric acid. In a typical procedure, 5mL of WFO (4.293g) was added into a 50-mL one-neckflask and heated to 100°C. After that, 1mL of concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) was dropped into the WFO under vigorous stir. The mixture was maintained at 100°C for 5min and then diluted with 30mL of deionized water, followed by adjusting the pH to 7 with NaOH. The suspension was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane to remove large carbon residues, yielding a dark yellowish-brown solution of S-C-dots. Further purification of the S-C-dots was conducted through a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut off: 1000Da) for 4days in the dark (changed deionized water every 8h). 0.997g of purified S-C-dots was finally obtained.

The quantum yield of the S-C-dots was measured using quinine sulfate as the standard and calculated with following equation:
QY
x
=
QY
std
(
I
x
/
I
std
) (
A
std
/
A
x
) (
η
x
2
/
η
std
2
)
where the subscript “x” designates the S-C-dots, the subscript “std” designates quinine sulfate, “QY” stands for the quantum yield, “I” stands for the integrated PL intensity, “A” stands for the absorbance, and “η” stands for the refractive index of the solvent. Quinine sulfate (quantum yield: 54%) was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index: 1.33) and the S-C-dots were dissolved in water (refractive index: 1.33). In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance value of the individual solution was kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength (360nm).

MTT assay was carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the S-C-dots on Hela cells. Hela cells at a density of 1×104 cells per well were first incubated at 37°C for 24h. After rinsing with phosphate buffered saline (PBS), Hela cells were incubated with 200μL culture medium containing the S-C-dots at different doses (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 600μgmL−1) for 48h. Then, the medium was removed and 20μL of MTT solution (5mgmL−1) was added. Hela cells were further incubated for 4h. Afterwards, the culture medium with MTT was removed and 200μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added, followed by shaking for 10min. The optical density of the mixture at 490nm was measured to assess the cell viability.

Hela cells were cultured at 37°C for 24h. Then, the cells were incubated with the S-C-dots at a concentration of 100μgmL−1 for 1h. After that, the cells were washed thoroughly three times with PBS. The cellular images were taken by a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SP5) and the emission was measured over the range of 450–550nm with excitation at 405nm.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and selected-area electron-diffraction (SAED) pattern were obtained with a JEM-2100 microscope operating at an accelerated voltage of 200kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed with a Shimadzu XRD-6000 powder X-ray diffractometer. Raman spectrum was recorded on a Horiba LabRAM ARAMIS Confocal Raman Microscope with a laser excitation of 532nm and a laser power of 6.4mW at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out with a PHI 5000 VersaProbe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Elemental analysis was achieved using a vario EL II analyzer. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum was collected on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer using KBr pressed disks. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were performed with a Bruker Avance III 600MHz spectrometer by dissolving 20mg of the S-C-dots in 700μL of D2O. Zeta potential was measured on a nano Z zeta potential analyzer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was taken on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired on a RF-5301PC spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetime was monitored using an Edinburgh FLS 920 time-resolved spectroscope. Cell imaging was done under Leica TCS SP5 confocal microscope with a laser excitation at 405nm.

WFO, mainly constituted by triglycerides [1], is hydrophobic and insoluble in water. The conversion of WFO into hydrophilic C-dots which contain unsaturated sp2 carbon clusters is a critical step. Concentrated sulfuric acid was selected as a carbonization agent for: (1) its strong dehydrating property which helps the formation of unsaturated CC from saturated C–C, and (2) its strong oxidizing property which assists the generation of hydrophilic C–O–H and OC–O–H from hydrophobic C–H. In order to obtain the desired products, a series of optimizationexperiments have been done. It was found that the reaction temperature was a vital parameter. Low temperature, for example 80°C, was not enough for complete carbonization of WFO even with a long reaction time (2h) because of insufficient reaction energy, while high temperature, for instance 120°C, leaded to over carbonization of WFO, immediately producing bulk black carbon residues. Thus, the moderate temperature, 100°C, was appropriate. The reaction yield of the S-C-dots was 23.2wt% with respect to the starting WFO, comparable with those of grass and hair fiber derived C-dots [48,53].

The morphology and structure of the S-C-dots were investigated by TEM, XRD and Raman measurements. TEM image (Fig. 2 a) shows that the S-C-dots are quasi-spherical and well-dispersed. They have a narrow size distribution between 1.0 and 4.0nm with an average diameter of 2.6nm (Fig. 2a, inset). No obvious lattice fringes are found in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of the S-C-dots (Fig. 2b), which is indicative of their amorphous nature. The indiscernible diffuse rings in the selected-area electron-diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2b, inset) also confirm the poor crystallization of the S-C-dots. The XRD pattern (Fig. 3 a) displays a broad, low-intensity diffraction peak centered at 23.6° with calculated interlayer spacing of 0.38nm. The larger interlayer spacing than that of bulk graphite (0.34nm) is attributed to the presence of abundant functional groups at the edges of the S-C-dots [19]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 3b) exhibits a D band at 1352cm−1 and a G band at 1584cm−1, corresponding to the sp3 and sp2 hybrid carbons respectively. The relative intensity of the characteristic bands (I D/I G) is 0.66, suggesting partial disordered graphite-like structure of the S-C-dots [5].

As shown in Fig. 6 a insets, the aqueous solution of the S-C-dots is yellowish and transparent in daylight but exhibits bright blue luminescence under excitation of 365nm UV light. The S-C-dots dispersed in water has a featureless UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 6a). The PL spectra of the S-C-dots (Fig. 6b) show the strongest emission peak at 378nm for excitation at 300nm, which is quite different from the reported doping-free or nitrogen-doped C-dots which always possess the strongest emission peak inthevisibleregion (>400nm). The blue-shifted PL is derived from the new emission centers related to sulfur-doping. Inotherwords, the successful sulfur-doping opens a wide band gap of 3.28eV in C-dots, producing an intense UV emission of 378nm. The emission of the S-C-dots depends on the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 520nm, the emission peak position shifts from 378 to 556nm (UV to yellowish-green) with the decrease of intensity. As the S-C-dots are nearly uniform in size, the excitation-dependent PL behavior is more probably attributed to the diverse surface emissive trap sites rather than the variation of particle sizes [24]. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curve (Fig. S4) can be fitted into a double exponential function with τ 1 of 2.28ns (48.9%), τ 2 of 9.89ns (51.1%) and average lifetime τ of 6.17ns. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the PL quantum yield of the S-C-dots is determined to be 3.66%, higher than the values of soot-derived C-dots (<1%) [42,43].

The PL intensity of the S-C-dots to the effects of UV exposure, ionic strength, and pH were investigated. No apparent decline of PL intensity is observed under continuous UV excitation for hours (Fig. S5) or in high concentration of NaCl (Fig. S6), suggesting the excellent optical stability of the S-C-dots. In contrast to the most pH-insensitive C-dots, the S-C-dots have a pH-sensitive feature. Fig. 7 a records the gradual enhancement of PL intensity in the pH range from 2 to 12. There is a nice linearrelationship between pH and PL intensity within pH 3–9 (Fig. 7b). It is found that the pH-response of PL intensity is reversible. The PL intensity increases when pH is tuned from 3 to 9 by adding NaOH, and afterwards, it decreases back to the initial value as pH is changed from 9 to 3 by dropping HCl. This process can be repeated several times (Fig. 7b, inset). Based on the above observations, we deduce that the PL intensity has relations with the protonation and deprotonation of the S-C-dots [58–60]. When pH increases from 3 to 9, the deprotonation degree of the S-C-dots increases, producing higher concentration of carboxyl groups on the surface of the S-C-dots which serve as fluorophore species, consequently enhancing the PL intensity. As the concentration of carboxyl groups approaches the maximum value, the increasing rate of PL intensity slows down (pH 9–12). This optically pH-sensitive property may render the S-C-dots attractive fluorescent probes for pH-monitoring in biological and biomedical applications.

MTT assay was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the S-C-dots before they were applied as fluorescent probes for bioimaging. The result proves that the S-C-dots are of low toxicity, for the reason that Hela cells keep viability at greater than 90% even incubated with the S-C-dots in the high concentration of 600μgmL−1 for 48h (Fig. S7). Cellular uptake of the S-C-dots was monitored by a confocal microscope. Hela cells treated with the S-C-dots show intense blue luminescence (Fig. 8 ), suggesting the as-synthesized S-C-dots are promising candidates of bio-labeling agent for living cells.

In conclusion, a facile method has been established to fabricate monodisperse S-C-dots by carbonization of WFO with the assistance of concentrated sulfuric acid. The successful sulfur-doping opens a wide band gap of 3.28eV, rendering the S-C-dots a strong UV emission of 378nm. Remarkably, the obtained S-C-dots show a pH-sensitive behavior and the PL intensity has a good linearrelationship within pH 3–9. Furthermore, possessing satisfactory PL quantum yield, high photostability, and low cytotoxicity, the as-prepared S-C-dots provide potential applications for bioimaging and beyond.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Basic Research Program (2010CB732401), National Science Fund for Creative Research Groups (21121091), National Natural Science Foundation of China (20875045).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.05.081.

Presence of amorphous carbon nanoparticles in food caramels

Simple and green synthesis of nitrogen-doped photoluminescent carbonaceous nanospheres for bioimaging
